topic: A college student discovers their roommate has been reading their journal
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of stale coffee and old paper usually grounded me, but today, it just made the buzzing in my temples worse. I walked into our shared room, the familiar clutter suddenly feeling sharp and hostile. My eyes immediately snagged on the open journal lying on my desk—my journal. The page was turned, not haphazardly, but deliberately, revealing a passage I’d only written a week ago, detailing a secret fear about my upcoming presentation. A cold, hollow feeling settled in my stomach, making my hands tremble as I reached for it. When my roommate, Alex, looked up from their laptop, their expression was neutral, almost bored. "What are you doing?" they asked, their voice flat. I couldn't form a coherent reply. I just stared at the ink, the world tilting slightly on its axis. Every sound in the room seemed amplified, and I felt a strange detachment, as if I were watching myself from a distance.

[story 2]

It started subtly. I found my sketchbook slightly askew, and when I looked closer, the page I’d been working on was annotated in a looping, unfamiliar hand. A knot tightened in my chest, a heavy, suffocating pressure that made breathing shallow. I tried to focus on my textbook, but my gaze kept drifting back to the desk. Alex was across the room, engrossed in their screen, a faint glow illuminating the corner of their face. Then I saw it: a bookmark, a thin strip of faded blue paper, tucked into the spine of my private diary. My breath hitched. My mouth went dry. I stood there, rooted to the spot, my feet feeling strangely heavy on the worn carpet. I wanted to bolt, but my limbs felt sluggish, unresponsive. The air in the room seemed thick, viscous, and I couldn't quite remember how to assemble a simple sentence.

[story 3]

The morning light streamed through the window, but it felt abrasive, stinging my eyes. I shuffled into the room, my movements jerky and uncoordinated. Something was wrong. My desk was subtly rearranged; the stack of notes I’d left beside the lamp was now neatly aligned, and the cover of my journal was slightly ajar. A wave of nausea washed over me, making the room spin. I approached the desk slowly, my heart hammering against my ribs like a trapped bird. When I finally looked down, the handwriting was undeniably mine, but interspersed with my own words were new entries, written with a distinct, knowing slant. A profound sense of being watched settled over me, a chilling awareness that I was no longer entirely in control of my own space. I backed away, bumping against the bookshelf, and whispered a sound that caught in my throat, unable to articulate the sheer strangeness of the situation.
